“Both parents have achondroplasia”
Known fact: Achondroplasia = autosomal dominant trait
If both parents are affected (heterozygous), their children will inherit:
- 25% chance of AA (usually lethal)
- 50% chance of Aa (affected)
- 25% chance of aa (unaffected)
So unaffected babies = 25%.

We cautiously apply minimal extrapolation consistent with core medical science needed to understand such queries.
Thus:
Clinical reasoning: Achondroplasia is caused by mutations in FGFR3 and inherited in an autosomal dominant manner. When both parents are affected and heterozygous, each child independently inherits either mutant or wild-type alleles. The probability of receiving two copies of the wild-type allele (resulting in an unaffected child) is 25%. While this detail isn’t explicitly covered in the provided texts, it aligns with established medical genetics principles underlying familial skeletal dysplasias.
Answer: \boxed{B}
(Note: Strictly speaking, without mentioning genetics or inheritance mechanisms in the source materials, this relies on external knowledge. However, given that no alternative explanation exists within the document corpus and that B is supported by universal medical teaching for this condition, it remains the most reasonable choice under practical clinical examination settings.)
